{"step_by_step_thinking": "To determine which muscle achieves retraction of the mandible, we analyze the functional roles of the listed muscles based on the provided documents.

- The temporalis muscle is described in multiple documents (e.g., Document [6], [9], [10]) as originating from the temporal fossa and inserting on the coronoid process of the mandible. Its contraction is stated to cause elevation and retraction of the mandible (Document [6], [9]). This directly matches the query.

- The medial pterygoid muscle is described as assisting in elevation and protrusion of the mandible (Document [6], [7], [27]), and is not specifically noted for retraction.

- The masseter muscle is described as elevating and protruding the mandible (Document [6], [9]), not retraction.

- The lateral pterygoid muscle is described as depressing the mandible and opening the mouth (Document [26]), and is involved in protrusion and lateral movements, not retraction.

Therefore, the only muscle explicitly described as causing retraction of the mandible is the temporalis muscle.", "answer_choice": "B"}
